claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who is 12, and Blake, who is 6.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children - Alex (age 12) and Blake (age 6). 

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, actually Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake lives with you, but Alex doesn't.

Can you tell me more about Alex's situation? Where does Alex live, and do you make any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent. I do help out financially - I contribute towards things like clothes and pocket money each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Alex, I need to understand the amount you're contributing.

How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each week? For reference, the current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, and £16.95 per week for additional children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £20 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. One important question: is the other parent (who Alex lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and nobody else is claiming it either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now, regarding Blake who lives with you - I just need to confirm a few things:

Is Blake in any of the following situations:
- In hospital or residential accommodation for an extended period?
- In local authority care?
- Or are there any other unusual circumstances affecting Blake's living situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake is fostered - I'm fostering Blake. The local authority gives me maintenance for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This is a key detail for Blake's eligibility.

Can you clarify: is the local authority paying anything specifically towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the council pays for Blake's maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me evaluate each child's situation:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Unfortunately, based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**.

**For Alex (age 12):**
- Although you contribute £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is less than the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child
- Since Alex doesn't live with you, you would need to be contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate to be considered responsible for them
- Because your contribution falls short of this threshold, you don't meet the eligibility criteria for Alex

**For Blake (age 6):**
- While you are fostering Blake, the local authority is paying towards their maintenance
- The rules state that foster carers can only claim Child Benefit if the local council is **not** paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance
- Since the council is providing maintenance payments, you're not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if you increase your contributions for Alex to at least £25.60 per week, or if the fostering arrangement changes for Blake), you may wish to reassess your eligibility.

reasonings: Alex is 12 years old and lives with their other parent, not with you. While you do contribute £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is less than the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child. Under the rules, you would usually be responsible for a child if you live with them OR if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since Alex doesn't live with you and your contribution (£20/week) is below the Child Benefit rate (£25.60/week), you do not meet the criteria to be considered responsible for Alex. Additionally, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, but you would need to meet the eligibility criteria yourself. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 6 years old and you are fostering them. According to the rules, you can get Child Benefit if you foster a child, BUT only as long as the local council is NOT paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. You have confirmed that the local authority does pay for Blake's maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because the local council is paying towards their maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have two children you're asking about. For Alex (age 12), who lives with their other parent, your weekly contribution of £20 is below the required Child Benefit threshold of £25.60, so you don't meet the criteria for being responsible for them. For Blake (age 6), whom you are fostering, you cannot claim Child Benefit because the local authority is paying towards their maintenance. Under the fostering rules, Child Benefit is only available to foster carers when the local council is not paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.

You have confirmed that the local authority does pay for Blake's maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because the local council is paying towards their maintenance.

For Blake (age 6), whom you are fostering, you cannot claim Child Benefit because the local authority is paying towards their maintenance. Under the fostering rules, Child Benefit is only available to foster carers when the local council is not paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.
